entry.title: Re: Meet Your Ops Agents - Powered by SAP Automation Pilot and Joule
entry.content:
Hello @Necronomicon !

Thank you for raising this question. The capability to create and use MCP server in a no-code manner in SAP Automation Pilot is provided out of the box. However, you will need to have the needed automations in the SAP Automation Pilot (of course, it comes with 300+ ready-to-use automations and also it provides options to extend these to meet pretty much any type of ops automations needs). 

The idea of outsourcing MCP servers in our Github repo is also a valid one. Our team will investigate it further and I will keep you posted about it. If we do so , I will also publish a blogpost about it. 

Moreover, can you please let us know what type of MCP servers / Ops Agents  you would be interested in to learn more about and / or to be open-sourced?

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: SmartRecruiters and SF Recruiting coexistence – Create Job from Position
entry.content:
Hello team,

We have reviewed the available documentation regarding the coexistence of SmartRecruiters and SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting (legacy) during a transition phase. The documentation generally mentions the possibility of running both solutions in parallel to support a smooth transition.

However, we could not find clear information on whether this coexistence can be driven by population or position.

More specifically, when using Position Management in SAP SuccessFactors and creating a recruiting request from the org chart (Create Job / Create Requisition from Position), how is the target system determined?

Is there any standard way to configure, for example at position, job, or organizational level, whether the recruiting request should be created in SmartRecruiters or in SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting?

In other words, is it possible to have different populations or job categories routed to different recruiting systems (SmartRecruiters vs SF Recruiting), or is the target system globally defined once the integration is in place?

We would appreciate confirmation of the standard behavior and any recommendations you may have for managing this situation during a transition phase.

Thank you in advance for your guidance.

Kind regards,
Barbara

entry.title: Troubleshooting Made Easy: Dive into Cloud Connector’s Built-in Diagnostics
entry.content:
Hello Cloud Connector enthusiasts!

If you’ve ever stared at a stubborn connectivity issue wondering “Why won’t this tunnel connect?” or “What’s blocking my RFC calls?”, you’re in good company. The Cloud Connector has a powerful, often underutilized feature right in the admin UI: the Troubleshooting page under Connector > Troubleshooting. This isn’t just a log viewer—it’s an intelligent diagnostics framework that detects, analyzes, and even suggests fixes for common issues. Here’s how it works, what it covers, and how to make it part of your daily routine.

What is the Troubleshooting Framework?

Certain types of issues—think tunnel timeouts, email alerts failing, or backend connection hiccups—are automatically detected and processed by the framework. They appear as diagnoses in a handy table on the Troubleshooting screen. Each diagnosis summarizes the problem and provides likely causes and suggested fixes to help you act fast.

Important note: These causes and fixes are hints, not guaranteed solutions. Always verify in your environment.

Key details in each diagnosis

Open any entry to drill into the details. For recurring issues, the framework won’t spam your table—it increments an occurrence counter instead. In the details pop-up (Actions column), you’ll see:

  • Creation Date: When the issue was first recorded.
  • Removal Date Unless Recurring Earlier: Many diagnoses auto-delete 24 hours after the last occurrence. No repeat? Poof—gone!
  • Minimal, Maximal, and Average Recurrence Interval: Shown for issues occurring multiple times within 24 hours—great for spotting patterns (e.g., “every 5–10 minutes”).
  • Technical Exception: Type and message (for example, io.netty.channel.ConnectTimeoutException).

You can delete diagnoses manually, but if the issue recurs, it’ll reappear with the counter reset to 1.

Alerting integration: Stay notified without the noise.

Background issues (like failing incoming requests) can generate alerts, which—depending on Alerting > E-Mail Configuration—may trigger automatic emails.

  • New diagnoses alert once; recurrences don’t spam your inbox.
  • Want to skip emails for new troubleshooting diagnoses? Uncheck “Troubleshooting” under Observation Configuration.
  • Deleting a diagnosis doesn’t clear the related alert—remove that manually if needed.
  • Since auto-recovery isn’t always detectable, some diagnoses and alerts can linger until the 24-hour timeout or manual cleanup.

What’s already covered? A look under the hood

The framework already detects and diagnoses several high-impact scenarios:

Tunnel problems

  • Connection timeout (io.netty.channel.ConnectTimeoutException): The Cloud Connector can’t establish in time the tunnel to the SAP Connectivity Proxy either in your BTP or in the SAP Solution which deployed the Connectivity Proxy. Find more information in the documentation. Typical causes include firewall rules, proxy misconfiguration, or network latency.

Alert email failures

  • Sender address rejected (“You are not authorized to send as…”): Check that the configured sender matches an authorized account on your mail server.
  • SMTP connection failure (“Could not connect to SMTP host: …”): Verify host, port, credentials, and whether your network/firewall allows outbound SMTP traffic.

RFC requests

  • Incomplete or erroneous Access Control configuration: RFC calls fail when the target Function Module isn’t allowed in Access Control or the ABAP backend connection is misconfigured. A classic “works in browser, fails in RFC” situation—double-check your Access Control entries.

Internal host connection tests

When you run a connection test to an internal (on-premises) host, you might see:

  • Host unknown: DNS can’t resolve the configured hostname.
  • Service or logical port unknown: The specified port/service isn’t reachable or recognized.
  • Backend unreachable / Connection refused / Timeout: The target system isn’t responding as expected.
  • Certificate not accepted: TLS/SSL handshake fails because the backend’s certificate isn’t trusted by the Cloud Connector.

How to use it in your daily workflow

  1. Navigate: Cloud Connector UI > Connector > Troubleshooting.
  2. Scan the table: Sort by recency or occurrences. Prioritize high counters.
  3. Drill down: Open entries to review causes and suggested fixes. Apply changes (e.g., revalidate access controls, adjust firewall rules).
  4. Monitor alerts: Pair with email settings for proactive notifications.
  5. Clean up: Delete resolved entries and watch for recurrences.
  6. Escalate smartly: Capture exception screenshots and collect logs before opening an SAP Support ticket.

When it doesn’t cover your issue

The framework focuses on common, auto-detectable configurations. For deeper analysis:

  • Check the Trace tabs (e.g., Tunnel, RFC) for detailed request/response data.
  • Review Account > Connectivity Tests for subaccount health.
  • Consult the SAP Help Portal: Cloud Connector Troubleshooting for comprehensive guidance.

The Troubleshooting page is a quiet time-saver. Before diving into traces or opening a ticket, spend 30 seconds in Connector > Troubleshooting. There’s a good chance the framework already flagged the issue and has a hint ready for you. Better yet, it’s passive—no agents or extra setup. It’s always on and keeps getting smarter as new issue types are added over time.

Have you uncovered a gem with the Troubleshooting framework? Share your experience in the comments!

Stay connected,
Your Cloud Connector Team

entry.title: Managing Parent-Child Hierarchies in SAC: A Datasphere-Driven Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

Setting up Public Dimensions correctly is a core requirement for any successful SAC Planning project. While Seamless Planning already positions SAP Datasphere as the central source for fact data, the same does not yet apply for master data. I will share key lessons learned from real-world implementations and show how to leverage Datasphere to avoid SAC pitfalls - from data acquisition to visualization.

In this example, a new cost center dimension is created with a parent-child hierarchy. The hierarchy loads into SAC and displays correctly in the story. Standard features such as disaggregation work perfectly out of the box. Up to this point, everything appears straightforward.

SAC Hierarchy Management

 

Hierarchy in Story

 

Empty Nodes

Issues arise when hierarchies change over time or contain empty nodes. One challenge is that SAC automatically creates hierarchy nodes as new dimension members. In typical S/4HANA scenarios, these nodes are text nodes rather than actual master data members. SAC also does not request a node type, making it difficult to distinguish between real dimension members and text nodes.

In the example below, the hierarchy node “Marketing” is replaced by “Administration.” Since “Marketing” no longer exists in the source, it is not updated and remains in the SAC hierarchy. Because it no longer has children, SAC treats it as a leaf member and marks it as input-enabled. As a result, disaggregation may push values into a text node that no longer exists in the source system - an outcome that rarely makes sense:

Orphaned Node

A simple workaround for empty nodes - whether they are empty in the source or become empty because their children move - is to explicitly define node types using a dimension property.

By introducing a property such as NodeType, you can classify members as:

  • L = Leaf
  • N = Node
  • R = Root

If your source is S/4HANA, this information is available directly (at least for hierarchies created via "Manage Global Hierarchies" app). For other sources, the SAP HANA hierarchy developer guide provides useful SQLScript expressions to derive node types.

When applying a filter such as NodeType = "L" in the story, empty nodes are hidden - even with Show Unbooked enabled - and disaggregation no longer targets these nodes.

Input Sheet with Property Filter

 

Leaf Members Leaving the Hierarchy

Another challenge occurs when a hierarchy loses leaf members. In this example, the leaf member “M_C” is no longer part of the hierarchy. SAC only updates members that appear in the source. If “M_C” is missing from the source, it remains under its previous parent node (here: “Administration”), even though it should no longer belong there. The question is: how do we ensure that “M_C” correctly leaves the hierarchy?

With Seamless Planning and Datasphere, the SAC Public Dimension table can be exposed to a Datasphere space. Although hierarchy columns are not part of the exposed master data table, we can get the information about existing members in SAC:

Exposed in Datasphere

 

 

Once the current dimension members are available in Datasphere, the hierarchy load can be enhanced with a simple SQL statement that also updates members that are not present in the source. For these members, the SQL logic can clear the parent column, ensuring they correctly leave the hierarchy.

Unfortunately, the load mechanism sets members as root, when they are part of the data source and left blank in the parent node:

Blank becomes <root>

 

So, leaf member "M_C" actually doesn't leave the hierarchy, it's just not part of the "TOTAL" node anymore. 

 

Conclusion

SAC dimensions with hierarchies can be created quickly, but challenges arise when loading hierarchies via external sources:

  • text nodes without children
  • text nodes removed from the source
  • leaf members removed from the source

Each of these issues can be addressed without overly complex solutions when Datasphere with Seamless Planning is used as the middleware. However, the effort and potential for errors should not be underestimated.

Even when all recommended measures are implemented, two limitations remain: First, orphaned nodes cannot be deleted from an SAC Public Dimension through a load process. Because SAC does not allow external deletion of master data records, these members remain in the dimension as unused entries. Second, former leaf members cannot truly be removed from the hierarchy through the load process. Instead, they can only be converted into root members, which may lead to additional clean-up effort or modeling inconsistencies.

Overall, the SAC hierarchy concept works very well when hierarchies are built directly within SAC. However, loading and maintaining hierarchies from external systems quickly becomes complex. Certain aspects (such as hierarchy sorting) are still not sufficiently supported. Hopefully, future enhancements to Seamless Planning - especially using Datasphere views as sources for SAC Public Dimensions - will simplify hierarchy loadling and provide a clearer distinction between true dimension members and hierarchy metadata.

entry.title: Re: ECP connectivity with MBC (Mutli Bank Connectivity)
entry.content:
Hi all,

Just to add some clarification here.
SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) can be integrated with SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity, but it is not a plug‑and‑play scenario like SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The integration requires specific payroll add‑ons and a supported technical setup, and the exact components depend on the ECP release and deployment model.
There is no single public “end‑to‑end” guide comparable to the S/4HANA Cloud best practices. The recommended approach is:
- Ensure the required payroll add‑ons/support packages are in place on the ECP side
- Secure the SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity license
- Engage the SAP MBC onboarding team, who will validate the landscape and guide the supported integration approach
For this reason, detailed technical documentation is typically shared during onboarding rather than published as a generic Help Portal guide.
If you are proceeding, raising a formal MBC onboarding request is the best next step to get confirmation for your specific ECP setup.

Regards
Mahendra C
MBC Team

entry.title: Re: Managing Parent-Child Hierarchies in SAC: A Datasphere-Driven Approach
entry.content: Great breakdown, @Tano_B! The issue of SAC treating removed nodes as input-enabled leaf members is a real headache for planning accuracy.Your workaround using a NodeType property (L, N, R) to filter the Story is a clever, low-complexity fix. It’s a great example of how Datasphere can act as the 'logic engine' to clean up SAC Public Dimensions when the source system falls short.Looking forward to future SAP updates that might finally allow for the external deletion of these orphaned records!
